old_claim.note: Supply Chain Management Software supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim_absent: No direct supply-chain-management software dependency remains because the scoped 1997 capability is supported by GPS receivers, electronic maps, vehicle tracking, routing, and dispatch without requiring a broader supply-chain suite.

source_shape.source_locator: Commercial Applications and Productivity Enhancement sections: attributes vehicle navigation, tracking, fleet management, routing, and dispatch gains to GPS receivers and electronic maps, without identifying supply-chain-management software as a constituent.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The contemporaneous federal account supports the fleet capability through GPS positioning, map displays, vehicle operations, and dispatch functions rather than an enterprise supply-chain platform.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A later fleet system may integrate with broader supply-chain software, but optional enterprise integration does not explain the documented 1997 GPS navigation and tracking milestone.

ontology_before: GPS-guided fleet logistics inherited a generic enterprise-software scaling edge unrelated to its documented positioning and dispatch mechanism.
ontology_after: The scoped node depends directly on GPS and motorized fleet operations; broader supply-chain software remains an optional downstream integration.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-gps-navstar-logistics-boundary: supply_chain_management_software is absent as a direct prerequisite of gps_guided_logistics.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to an integrated supply-chain execution suite whose documented operation requires supply-chain-management software.
The 1997 source is shown to make an enterprise supply-chain platform constitutive of the described GPS fleet functions.
short_reason: The documented GPS fleet capability does not require a broader supply-chain software suite.
